     The Abominable Oracle (42 Augurs): A complete card game for traversing our 7 Worlds—ready to undergo its transformation into a Gold Edition as stretch goals are unlocked.
     The Book of Augurs: The illustrated guide that recounts the story of each Augur and introduces the iconic figures of our universes, accompanied by the intuitive insights they inspire.
     The Oculus Malignus Boosters (Optional): 14 spell cards (7 "Forces of Interference" from Graooum—our monster-guide—and 7 new Augurs named "Illusions") to spice up your games and summon misfortune.

Australia is home to some of the world's most unique flora and fauna. Our native bees in particular are highly diverse and poorly understood. Did you know that most Aussie bees are solitary, don't nest in hives, or even make honey? Unfortunately native bees are under severe threat from habitat loss, and the first step to making people care about them is to get to know them first!

Bee The Buzz teaches about some native Australian bees in an entertaining and engaging manner. Players take turns to literally fly their bees - by throwing bee cards to try and land on flowers and Bee Hotels to score (these are things that help support bee habitats). At the same time, try and avoid pesticides and other human activities that hurt bees. Of course, no game is complete without some ways to buzz your opponents away! Use Pollen Power cards to gain an advantage - such as the Blindfold card that forces a player to close their eyes during their throw attempt.

Packed with practical information such as what flowers attract certain types of bees, players learn as they play. Soon you'll be a bee expert and be the buzz around town!

—description from the designer

boardgame: River Valley Jewelcraft: Mastercrafts and Other Fineries
description:
New gems have been discovered. As such, some more burrowing critters have moved to town to join in on the rush!

Mastercrafts and Other Fineries lets players mix and match up to three new modules. With Extra Explorers, add the new characters, including the Hedgehogs. These clever siblings are represented with multiple worker meeples, spreading out across the board and meeting up at their Nest for big bonuses.

Earrings adds a new jewelry type, for additional scoring variety and strategy.

Finally, Masterpieces provides each player with a powerful, private jewelry card to craft. These are important cards that will completely change your strategies.

The death of the great Osiris is shrouded in legend. After the passing of this beloved king of Egypt, the struggle for succession between Horus and Set escalated until it reached the moment of final judgement. In the solar palace at Heliopolis the two factions tug ceaselessly back and forth, while the assembled gods stand watching the trial unfold. Ra, the sun god, wishes to court the formidable might of Set without alienating the many followers of Horus, and so takes upon himself the role of mediator, striving to keep the scales in balance.

As a servant of the gods, you will work to sway the tide of events and help your chosen deities gain the upper hand. You may seize the opportunity to declare a prophecy, but above all you must secretly swear your allegiance — for when the struggle is finally settled, all of your efforts will be repaid in glory beyond measure.

Gods of Egypt is played over three rounds, each consisting of ten tricks. The card that wins each trick advances the Influence marker according to its direction and value. At the end of every round, players score points based on the final position of the Influence marker (through successful allegiance and successful prophecies). After three rounds, the player with the highest total score wins. In the event of a tie, all tied players share the victory.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: カスタムスロットミリオネア (Custom Slot Millionaire)
description:
Transform an ordinary slot machine into a gorgeous machine that ignites the desire for gambling, and capture the hearts of your customers!

This is a game where you customize slot machines. Spin the slots to earn VP (Victory Points), and as the rounds progress, you can make spectacular modifications to your slots! You can increase the multiplier, remove specific outcomes, rearrange the positions of the outcomes, increase the number of spins...the possibilities for modifications are endless! Furthermore, you can earn even more VP by predicting which slot machine will have the highest output.

Spin the slot machine over and over, experimenting and refining your design, and installing custom parts to create a one-of-a-kind machine. Now, let's spin the slots and aim to become a millionaire!

boardgame: Restart: Jacob and Sophie Miniature Pack
description:
The Veteran and The Girl With a Plan are two extra time travelers with unique powers.

—description from campaign

Upgrade your gameplay with the Gadgets Expansion, designed to bring more variety, strategy, and unexpected twists to your games of Restart.

This add-on introduces a full set of Gadget cards, each tailored to the different Dystopias, giving players access to unique abilities, tactical advantages, and clever ways to outsmart their opponents.

Each Dystopia features 14 Gadget cards (consisting of 7 unique cards, each included twice) ensuring both balance and strategic consistency for all playstyles.

What’s inside:
56 Gadget Cards (44 x 68 mm)
2 Rules Cards for quick reference
All cards are printed on 300 gsm blue core paper, ensuring durability and a premium feel during play.
All expansion cards fit inside the game box, even when sleeved.

Perfect for players who want more interaction, replayability, and dynamic decision-making, the Gadgets Expansion can smoothly fit into the base game.

Take control. Adapt. Outsmart. Restart... with Gadgets.

Gadgets are acquired through a dynamic drafting phase at the start of the game. Shuffle all Gadget cards and reveal two per player, plus two additional cards. Beginning with the first player and proceeding clockwise, players may purchase one Gadget by paying its Adrenaline crystal cost or choose to pass. After the initial round, the last player has the option to acquire a second, different Gadget, and the selection continues in reverse order. Each Gadget provides a unique ability, offering powerful strategic options that can boost your prosperity when used at the right moment.

—description from campaign

Expand your strategy with the Duality Mini Expansion, an add-on that introduces new ways to build patterns and optimize your decisions in Restart.

This mini expansion includes a set of Duality Event cards, each combining two different colors. These versatile cards can be used as either color when creating patterns, giving you greater flexibility and opening up new tactical opportunities.

Whether you're adapting to the board or planning ahead, Duality cards reward creative thinking and smart timing.

What’s inside:
6 Duality Event Cards (65 x 100 mm)
1 Rule Card
All cards are printed on 300 gsm blue core paper, ensuring durability and a premium feel during play.
All expansion cards fit inside the game box, even when sleeved.

A small addition with a big impact, the Duality Mini Expansion enhances replayability and adds more flexible choices to every game of Restart.

Duality cards can be added into the game during setup. Each player starts with one Duality Event card in addition to their initial hand, while the rest are shuffled into the Event deck. These versatile cards function like standard Event cards, but with added flexibility: when played on the Spacetime grid, you may choose one of their two benefits, while they simultaneously count as both colors for pattern creation. When used on your Mind board, they behave as normal Event cards, often allowing you to access powerful actions earlier in the game.

—description from campaign

Chicago, 1920s. The city is yours to take — if you're ruthless enough.

In this competitive deck-building and area-control game for 2–4 players, you lead a criminal gang fighting for dominance over Chicago's neighborhoods during Prohibition. Control the streets, run your Speakeasies, sell alcohol, and outmaneuver your rivals — all while staying one step ahead of the police and the relentless Eliot Ness.

How it plays: Each of the 4 rounds is divided into five phases. During Disposition, alcohol barrels are placed across the city, police are deployed, an Event card shakes up the situation, and players recruit Associates and draw their hand. During Execution, players take turns choosing between playing cards to perform an Action or drawing extra cards to reorganize. Actions include sending Thugs to neighborhoods, moving units, selling alcohol, running a drive-by shooting, placing vehicles, bribing your way to control, or recovering Thugs from prison. After acting, players may complete a Job from the open market for ongoing bonuses. The Detention phase sees the police and Eliot Ness sweep their adjacent neighborhoods, confiscating barrels and sending Thugs to prison. During Extortion, each player collects income from their Speakeasies. Finally, Progression advances the round tracker and rotates the first player.

Winning: After 4 rounds, players count their dollars: cash behind their screen, Job card values, Influence converted to money, Cars on the streets, minus a penalty for Thugs still in prison. The richest gang boss wins.

Can you build the most powerful criminal empire in Chicago — or will Eliot Ness shut you down?

—description from the publisher

boardgame: 1833 1/3: Rivalry and Conflict in the Home Counties
description:
This, the third instalment in Mike Hutton's train game series is a collaboration with Tim Franklin.

Modelled on Francis Tresham's 1829, and other games in the 18xx series, the game is set in Kent, Sussex and Surrey in the UK. Players invest in railway companies and compete to out-muscle each other in the cauldron of early British railway rivalry. But this is no place for friendly rivalry. When a town is fully occupied, you are locked out of it unless you have a friendly station token there. You can't even upgrade it to muscle your way in. You may be forced to bypass the town or city entirely.

In a major departure from other games in the series, share buying and operating companies happen in the same round. The order of companies is determined by how well they did last time. They perform their usual duties, but maintain their rolling stock at the start of their turn by upgrading their train to a better one, or paying a maintenance charge on the one they currently have. This maintenance cost ramps up to eye-watering proportions as the game progresses. The game includes a number of other innovative mechanisms for players to explore and exploit.

Revenues grow quickly, but so do running costs. Company directors need to keep investors happy, as failing to give them their due may result in hostile takeovers. If players fail to give the market its due, an investor revolt occurs, and market consolidation will force weaker companies to merge, whether players like it or not. Managing and anticipating these unpredictable changes in fortune will allow you to carve a path through the maelstrom and emerge victorious!

—description from the designer

Tumbles is a tabletop mashup of lawn bowls, curling, and many other "boules" game. Players take on the roles of "Winds" trying to push their Tumbles along to the last drop of Water and help them thrive.

A Tumble is a small, plastic spheroid designed with a specific bias. This allows them to always lean in one particular direction when rolled and creates some interesting tactical opportunities. The Water is a marble that rests in the middle of the playing surface, which should be something like a desk mat or very long mousepad. Players alternate turns trying to get their Tumbles closest to the Water. After all eight of both players' Tumbles are rolled, whoever is closest scores 1 point for each of their Tumbles that are closer. After four or eight ends, whichever player has more points wins. As in curling, the player who doesn't score in the previous end "has the hammer" and goes second in the following end, a major advantage.

This game is also a small experiment in open source games. All the files to 3D print the components are freely available. Customization, experimentation, and modification are not only allowed, but highly encouraged. The likelihood of any two sets coming out exactly the same is small, which means playing around with different materials is part of the fun.

—description from the designer

SNAP is a fast-playing, pocket-sized football card game for 2 players set in the FIRST DOWN universe.

Players take turns running drives through a mix of hidden card selection, dice resolution, weather effects, and positional bonuses. At the start of each drive, a weather card modifies the conditions. The offense then chooses a featured position, QB, RB, or WR, which gains a bonus for the drive but may also signal the offensive plan to the defense.

On each play, offense and defense secretly choose cards, reveal simultaneously, roll the dice, and resolve the result using the card values, weather modifier, and position bonus. The first player to score 3 touchdowns wins.

SNAP includes Offense Cards, Defense Cards, Weather Cards, Trash Talk Cards, a Quick Reference Card, 2 dice, and a DRIVE BLOCK score pad. It can be played as a standalone card game, a quick game-night opener, a tailgate game, or a warm-up to FIRST DOWN.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Rocket Ranchers: Even MORE Space Cats!
description:
Space cats are very curious creatures, which means they can get in the way sometimes!

This promo includes 9 new Space Cat Cards and an alternate Space Cat Scoring Card to mix up your game of Rocket Ranchers!

—description from the designer

(ENG)
In Cubicópolis, each player represents an architecture studio whose members must train in design disciplines in order to fulfill project commissions and construct buildings of varying complexity in the city.
Throughout the game, each studio will gain prestige as it meets different objectives, and—after a year of hard work—the one with the greatest reputation will be the winner.
Mechanically, it is a management and dice placement game through which players must manage resources, fulfill contracts, construct buildings, and be the first to reach certain goals.

(ESP)
En Cubicópolis, cada jugador representa a un atelier de arquitectura cuyos integrantes deben formarse en las disciplinas de diseño a fin de poder cumplir con encargos de proyectos y construir edificios de distinta complejidad en la ciudad.
A lo largo del juego cada atelier irá ganando prestigio a medida que vaya cumpliendo con los diferentes objetivos y - al cabo de un año de arduo trabajo - aquel con mayor reputación será el ganador de la partida. 
Mecánicamente se trata de un juego de gestión y colocación de dados a través de los cuales los jugadores deben administrar recursos, cumplir contratos, construir edificios y ser los primeros en alcanzar determinadas metas.

—description from the designer

The trading post of Furha is in flames. Thick smoke hangs in the air, burned out market stalls and lifeless bodies bear witness to the massacre that took place here. Many traders were killed, others fled in panic. The bandits looted everything they could carry and retreated to their hideout at Garnet Mountain.

You are an assassin in the service of Star Money, a shadowy figure devoted to purging evil from the land. Your mission is clear: infiltrate the bandits’ hideout and kill their leader, Torben.

Grimm Delve is a solo Roll & Write game in which you explore an ever‑growing, dangerous cave system. Step by step, new chambers emerge from the darkness as you push deeper into the hideout. Each round represents a careful advance: you chart rooms connected to your current location, move into unexplored territory, face whatever dangers or opportunities await within, and then extend the twisting corridors even further into the mountain. Along the way, you will fight bandits, gather equipment, and struggle to preserve both your life and the light of your torch.

Victory comes only if you defeat the bandit leader lurking within the depths. Failure awaits if your wounds become fatal, if your torchlight fades and leaves you lost in the darkness, or if the cave itself defeats you—when no undiscovered room remains to be entered, your hunt comes to an end.

Descend carefully. Every step may bring you closer to Torben… or to your own demise.

—description from the designer

In Who's in the Bushes?, a deadly Predator is stalking your group — and your so-called friends will throw you to the wolves without a second thought. On your turn as the hunted player, you must either play a card from your hand to redirect the chase or blindly draw from another player's hand and hope for the best. Escape and the Predator moves on. Get caught and you draw from the fate deck — survive or get eliminated. The last player standing wins.

—description from the designer

A collectible trading card game for 2-4 players that uses a single deck of 45 cards. Players take turns playing mood cards to score the most points at the end of each round. The first player to win 3 rounds is the winner of the game.

Mood Swings has fun card interactions, adaptability, collecting, and trading—but in the style of a traditional card game, something you could buy off the shelf and play immediately.

While there's no necessary deck building, it's still a trading card game, which means each deck is not the same. You will get 45 cards (23 common, 14 uncommon, 6 rare, and 2 mythic rare cards), all randomized. If you go to your friend's house and play their copy of Mood Swings, the cards won't all be the same. You'll recognize some of the cards, but others will be new to you. The game, as Magic's creator Richard Garfield likes to describe trading card games, is "bigger than the box," meaning each game uses some of the pieces but not all of them.

In total, Mood Swings has 133 cards—48 commons, 40 uncommons, 30 rares, and 15 mythic rares. Each copy of Mood Swings will include 45 of those 133 cards. The game uses Magic's five colors, and the art on the cards are all sketches from published Magic art, but the cards each represent an emotion or mood.

The follow-up, stand alone sequel to the runaway hit party game. All new questions and a full set of components for up to 12 players. These questions can be mix and matched with the original Green Team Wins. More options, more fun!

Get on the Green Team. Stay on the Green Team. And win. Again. And Again.

The game is played over 15 rounds, where players will simultaneously answer one question at a time.

There are three types of questions: Fill in the Blank, This or That, and Multiple Choice. To set up the game, randomly choose 5 cards from each type and shuffle them.

Everyone will be asked the same question, then writes down their responses at the same time. All players with the winning answer join the Green Team and score points. If your answer is not the most popular among the other players, then you join the Orange Team and score zero points for that round. Players joining the Green Team from the Orange Team will score one point. Players who are on the Green Team and stay on it will earn two points.

But it's not about having the best answer, or the smartest answer, or the funniest answer. Only the most popular answer wins - the one that the most players at the table wrote down.

To win, get on the Green Team, stay on the Green Team, and win - because that's what the Green Team does. They win.

—description from the publisher
family: Components: Dry Erase Markers / Dry Erase Surfaces

description:
Kartomantik is a strategic two-player card game that moves away from traditional annihilation goals, focusing instead on the deep understanding and assimilation of the opponent's soul. Each player’s deck is a faithful mirror of their inner self, where every card and its Temperament represents a fragment of that soul.

Game Contents:
- 300 Unique Cards, distributed across:
5 Base Temperament packs (50 cards each).
A Meta-Temperament pack (50 cards, containing Hollow/Transcendent Manifestations and Multi-Temperament/Hollow Wills).
- A 28 pages Rulebook, featuring an 8-page introductory comic to immerse you in the world of Kartomantik, along with 20 pages of rules.

Gameplay
Play Time: 30-60 min
The game unfolds through shared turns, revolving around the "Confrontation"—an intense exchange where the Cartomancers' souls are measured against each other. Every clash begins with the simultaneous revelation of a First Manifestation, representing an impulse, a fear, or a passion. To actively shape this dialogue, players utilize "Wills" cards, which are acts of pure intention capable of altering the course of the Confrontation.

When a player prevails in a Confrontation, they assimilate the opposing Manifestations into their "Empathic Vessel." Their own Manifestations may return to their deck or be placed in the "Interzone," from which it can impact the game long-term.
The game ends when one player's deck has been depleted. The winner is the one who has accumulated the most points in their Empathic Vessel, thus proving they have more deeply understood the inner self of their opponent.

Small Wars Historical & Fantasy is a tabletop wargame system designed to provide a standardised core ruleset for skirmish-scale battles across historical and fantasy settings, set prior to the widespread use of firearms. Small Wars enables players to quickly get models on the board and start playing with minimal preparation. The system serves as a flexible toolkit for creativity and experimentation. It offers a robust framework for building custom units, contingents, armies, and scenarios supporting everything from historical reenactment to fantasy conflicts.

—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: A.M.P Tactics: A Modular Mech Wargame
description:
A.M.P Tactics: Ground Operations

A.M.P Tactics is a fast-paced, high-stakes tabletop miniature wargame focused on tactical mech combat in a crumbling industrial future. In this world, victory isn’t just about having the biggest gun—it’s about managing heat, maximizing positioning, and pushing your machines to the absolute breaking point.

What is an A.M.P?
At the heart of the game is the Advanced Mobile Platform (A.M.P). These are not just mindless automatons; they are highly customizable, pilot-driven engines of war. From the agile Scout Platform and the elusive Hunter Drones to the planet-shaking Titan Platform, every A.M.P in your cadre is a unique tool designed for a specific tactical purpose.

Core Gameplay Features:

     The Comparative Engine: Say goodbye to static target numbers. A.M.P Tactics uses a dynamic Comparative Roll-Off system. Compare your unit's Ballistics against the enemy's Evasion, or your weapon's Power against their Armor. The better your tech relative to theirs, the more dice you roll.
     Risk vs. Reward (System Overload): Need that critical hit? You can Overload your systems to add extra dice to any roll. But beware: your reactor suffers heat stress, dealing unavoidable damage to your own chassis.
     Brutal Damage & Criticals: As your A.M.P takes damage and falls to half strength, its systems begin to fail. Critical Structure hits can sever weapon links, blow out leg servos, or trigger catastrophic reactor leaks.
     Asymmetric Modern Warfare: Command more than just mechs. Deploy Armored Infantry to hold objectives or launch Drone Swarms that operate on a different plane of engagement—untargetable by standard weapons unless you have the right electronic warfare modules.


Digital Tools Included

     The A.M.P Forge: A built-in digital list-builder that lets you swap modules, assign Commander Traits, and export your company manifest as a print-ready file or a JSON data-chip.
     Rogue Defense Grid (Solo Mode): Want to play alone or cooperatively? The Rogue Logic Engine provides fully automated AI programming that controls the enemy forces, allowing for challenging solo play without a human opponent.


Warfront Campaigns
Take your lance through a 10-Round Warfront Campaign. Manage a persistent supply pool, salvage wrecked parts from the battlefield, and watch your pilots grow from green recruits to legendary Ace Pilots with permanent stat bonuses—if they survive.

Product Specifications:

     Format: Digital Rulebook (HTML-based for easy cross-platform use and printing).
     Scale: 28mm - 32mm (Scale agnostic; works with any sci-fi miniatures).
     Play Area: 4' x 4' table (Optional Hex-Grid rules included for 2" hex mats).
     Game Length: 60–90 minutes.

Take to the void and fight for control of a fractured Solar System.

SolCorps: Starfighters is a fast-paced tabletop dogfighting game set in the 23rd Century, where the United Earth Colonial Rangers and the militant ConTek corporation clash across orbital space, planetary atmospheres, and the asteroid belt.

Command agile starfighters in dynamic, hex-based engagements where positioning, timing, and nerve decide the outcome. There are no damage tracks—every hit matters. Outfly your opponent, line up the perfect attack, and strike before they can react.

This core rulebook includes:

     Complete rules for fast, tactical starfighter combat
     Four starter starfighters across two factions
     A focused set of customisable special systems
     Scenario-based play across varied engagement zones
     Optional expanded and advanced rules for added depth


Simple to learn, quick to play, and full of meaningful decisions, SolCorps: Starfighters delivers cinematic dogfights without complexity getting in the way.

Will you outmanoeuvre your enemy… or be reduced to debris in the vacuum of space?

Serpentine is a small card-drafting, tile-laying game where you will play as an Explorer-Planner of the town of Serpentine, Western Australia.

It uses a serpentine (or snake) drafting system where players will draft cards and play them onto a growing map.

Each player will have a terrain type (town, farm or mountain) and line type (road, railway or river). At the end of the game, score your largest area and line. Highest combined score wins.

—description from the publisher

SolCorps: Colonial War is a gonzo homebrew combined arms wargame, including mechs!

It's rules-lite and quick to play and is full of risk/reward tactical decisions. It is all the rules, factions, and unit data you need to play tabletop skirmishes in an exploited solar system.

The game is minis agnostic - use tokens, any proxy you like, or build your units with plastic bricks.

This is the second Compendium for the game, a 96 page volume that includes:

     The basic rules.
     Army lists and datacards for the Wasteland Tribes and Spacer Clans.
     Army lists and datacards for two AI factions - Synth Unity and the Exiled.
     Lots of black and white and colour unit tokens for all the factions.
     The Specials Index, providing a card for every Special released to-date.


Turn Sequence:

     Place Mech Orders.
     Roll for Initiative.
     The player that won Initiative chooses which player has a unit


act first. That player then moves and makes attacks with one
unit.

     If an enemy unit is attacked and is still operational after attacks have been resolved, and has not yet acted, that unit may


then react. It may only attack the unit that attacked it.

     The opposing player moves and makes attacks with one unit that


has not acted or reacted.

     If an enemy unit is attacked and is still operational after attacks have been resolved, and has not yet acted, that unit may then react. It may only attack the unit that attacked it.
     Alternate between players until both players units have all


moved and attacked

     Resolve End Phase. This includes determining if the game


is concluded.

     Begin new Turn Sequence, if the game has not concluded.

As a donut-loving cop in the Sprinkleton PD, you have two objectives: Stash Donuts & Catch Baddies from the streets.

Explore locations around town in search of Donuts & Baddies. Along the way, gather the Equipment Cards needed to catch Baddies, use Action Cards to steal, attack & advance your game, and find Stash Cards to secure your donuts. The first player to reach the Total Donut Score, wins!

—description from the designer

Metabaloids is a tactical trading card game where players command biologically inspired creatures in a chaotic battle for survival. Each player builds a deck using a cost-based system and deploys creatures into structured battlefield formations.

The game features both a Core Tactical Mode with a grid-based play area and a Fast Multiplayer Mode designed for quicker sessions. Players must manage resources, position cards strategically, and activate abilities at key moments to gain the advantage in combat.

During battles, players combine attack and defense values across their formations while drawing cards to modify outcomes, creating dynamic and unpredictable engagements.

In multiplayer games, victory is determined by survival and efficiency, with players aiming to minimize losses while outlasting their opponents.

—description from the designer

A hybrid of a physical dexterity game and a strategic card game, Hit and Run is a lively party experience designed especially for cat lovers. Players take on the roles of mischievous cats attempting to “steal” food from a table.

The main objective is to use your unique kitty powers—represented by cards—while skillfully striking the game box with a special ball-on-a-stick “paw.” Your goal is to knock down three out of the six food items displayed on top. Each food item is a small, detailed figurine representing one of six different dishes, adding a playful and tactile element to the game.

Explore 200 strange moments from history while trying to put them in the right order. Surpass your friends or work together on subjects ranging from warfare and food to cinema and experimental medicine, while avoiding art forgeries and homonymous assassins.

All the cards in a series are shown on the table. In clockwise order, each player or team announces a year for the first card, the card is then revealed and each player scores. This is repeated for each card in the series, while taking turns the role of first speaker. Variants In competitive play, reveal a series and have both teams bet on how many cards they can get in the right order. The highest better sorts and then reveals the cards. If they have enough cards in the right spots, they win their bet in points.

A fast-paced (except when it’s not) race through the sequence of events of how the pandemic rolled out in NZ. Suitable for ages 5-105 years, it’s a roll and move snakes ‘n’ ladders style game but with added cards. It is non-political!
As you land on the bubbles, you follow the instructions. When asked to pick a card, you then might have an action to do, such as your favourite internet dance for 30 seconds, or laugh for 30 seconds, because laughter is the best medicine!
 The game is mostly luck, but there is a little strategy involved. Some of the cards have special power attached and you can keep these until you choose to wield that power. The toilet-roll card is collected for trade, or to give you a massive jump ahead. There are three ways to end, depending on how short or long a game you want.
Players have said it’s a real conversation starter and others that it has helped reframe that experience for them. Teachers have said it should be in schools, for social history. Everyone says it’s fun and it is a hit with kids of all ages. 
 5% of profits from sales donated to research into ME/CFS/Long COVID. 
Indie publisher with lived experience of ME/CFS :) “Uniting friends and families through fun and laughter”.

Players take turns spinning a 1-6 spinner, and then selecting a tile with the matching number of paws.

They reveal the tile and add it to the end of their dog.

Some tiles have tails, and which point the player's dog is complete. The person with the most spots on their dog at the end wins.

Most cards in a deck display a number from 1 to 10. Each card takes a multi-step journey from the deck to a player's hand, from which a player, on their turn, discards a card and then (i) draws a number of cards from the deck equal to the number displayed on the discarded card and places those drawn cards into a personal face-down resource line, (ii) flips the displayed number of cards in the resource line to face-up, (iii} adds the displayed number of cards from the face-up cards to their hand, or (iv) moves their pawn the displayed number of spaces up the mountain (scoring track). The first player to reach the top of the mountain wins. Many number cards come with rule-breaking abilities, as do the few special cards.

Cards display one of six Egyptian images. Players take turns rolling a die and drawing the rolled number of cards from the deck. Then, players place the cards face-up into their personal line unless a four or higher is rolled, in which case any cards in excess of three go into a common play area, where those cards may continue an existing group of like images or start a new group. Then the active player may "match" by adding a number of cards with the same image from their line to an equal number of such cards in the play area. Once a group in the play area reaches 7 cards, those cards are discarded. Play continues until the deck runs out, at which time every player's pawn advances ten spaces on a pyramid minus the number of leftover cards in the player's line. The next round starts, and the player whose pawn first reaches the top of the pyramid wins.

Clouds of War is a single-player card game that simulates the career of a pilot during the Great War.

The components consist of 54 cards and a printed map on which the player tracks their progress. The goal of the game is to become an ace and earn enough Prestige points to finish the game as a heroic figure.

Cards have multi-use and with them all situations in the game are resolved. Player uses his own cards face-down as they sport icons that activate his piloting skills.

Campaign last for 2 years, and by the end of the game scoring Prestige will show what kind of a 'win' player managed to achieve.

Each player starts with 100 points and a hand of five cards. On your turn, play two cards from your hand. Most cards show a boost value and a drop value, along with a + or x operation (boost) or a - or / operation (drop). Play the cards on yourself or on an opponent to adjust point totals. A score tracker is included to enable players to keep track of their points. A few cards allow players to alter their own or their opponents' hands instead of adjusting points. After each player takes a turn, replenish hands and play three more rounds. The winner is the player whose point total is nearest to 200 after four rounds.

Brace yourselves, 34 frosty new cards are crashing into your zoo!
Meet the Musk Ox, Wolverine, Arctic Lemming, Snowy Owl, and Lynx, plus two new Star Animals: the Polar Bear and Snow Leopard, freshly rescued from a Vegas lounge act.
Enjoy classic cards with icy twists, a brand-new Mission, and a Twist of Fate deck gone sub-zero.
Win the National Lottery, charm a Zoo Celebrity, or steal snacks with some Rough Trade raccoon-style chaos.
It’s snow joke, this expansion makes your zoo colder, bolder, and way more unpredictable.

Shih Tzu is game from independent games publisher Average Bear Games Ltd. 
Contact: hello@average-bear.games 
To find out more information or buy direct visit average-bear.games

Things are really heating up in the Arabian Desert Expansion: 41 sizzling new cards!
Five new animals join the fray, including the majestic Arabian Oryx, sly Red Fox, cheeky Baboon, tiny Jerboa, and frill-iant Frilled Dragon.
Chase down the powerful new Pack of Striped Hyenas, explore desert-ready remixes of classic cards, and tackle a new Mission.
Plus, three scorching Twist of Fate cards: land a Celebrity Sponsor, pull off a Wild Trade, or roll the dice with a Lucky Dip.
Can your zoo handle the heat?

Shih Tzu is game from independent games publisher Average Bear Games Ltd. 
Contact: hello@average-bear.games 
To find out more information or buy direct visit average-bear.games

Wade into the wild with the Pantanal Wetlands Expansion: 49 New Cards!
Introducing six weird and wonderful new creatures, including the snack-sniffing Giant Anteater, chill Capybara, slithering Anaconda, glorious Jabiru Stork and elusive Jaguar. Track down a new Star Animal that’s lost in the city.
Six classic cards get a wild makeover, plus there’s a new Mission and three game-changing Twist of Fate cards: win a rehab grant, earn Diversity Champion, or trigger a brutal Food Shortage on a rival zoo.
Get ready, things are about to get wet, wild, and wonderfully weird.

Shih Tzu is game from independent games publisher Average Bear Games Ltd. 
Contact: hello@average-bear.games 
To find out more information or buy direct visit average-bear.games

A 2-player spatial game in which your goal is to create two big groups of connected pieces. Your score at game's end is the product of the number of pieces in your two largest groups.

For example, if each of your two biggest groups has 10 pieces in it, your score is 100.

But if one of your biggest groups has 18 pieces in it, and the other has 2, your score is 36.

In each case, your biggest pair of groups contains the same total number of pieces, but the scores are very different.

Because you can choose to place either your pieces OR your opponent's pieces, you can threaten to connect your opponents biggest groups and collapse their score.

However the attempt comes at a cost: if you fail, you'll have added to their score. The game's tension lies in this dynamic.

—description from the designer

Paseo por la ciudad is an educational children's game that simulates a journey through a bustling city. Originally published in Germany as Rund um die Stadt by Ravensburger (Otto Maier Verlag), this Spanish edition was distributed by Educa.

The board depicts a detailed city map with various shops, landmarks, and public services. Players must navigate the streets to reach specific destinations assigned by cards. The game focuses on teaching children basic urban navigation, spatial awareness, and recognition of common city locations. By moving their pawns according to dice rolls, players learn to plan their routes while interacting with the different elements of the city environment. The first player to visit all their assigned locations and return to the starting point wins.

Various communities have their own versions of the moccasin game. A game historically played by men using pairs of moccasins. This game is not to replace its traditional counterpart, it is a teaching tool intended as an introduction to the game. We hope this version inspires you to seek out those in your community who carry the true game. Players are divided into a hunting team and a hiding team. Each team has 4 tiles with a moccasin on the front and a marble on the back. The hunting team needs to guess correctly on their third turn, which tile has a red marble on the back. The hiding team earns points by successfully hiding the red marble.

—description from the back of the box

A traditional Anishinaabe Board Game. Bagese (or the bowl game) is a Native American dice game played by the Ojibwe. The word Bagese is the participle form of the verb "to throw" in the Ojibwe language. It is in reference to the dice going in the air. It is usually played either by two individuals, or by two sets of players. This game is usually played for amusement. However, it was known to be a game for gambling as well. Bagese is played using eight counter of bone carved into various shapes (men, snakes, war clubs, fish, and ducks). These are thrown from a bowl and a score is derived from the way in which they fall, depending on which sides land uppermost.

—description from the back of the box

Spartan Speech is a word game where players try to give the shortest possible clues to help a guesser identify a specific answer.

Each round, one player is the guesser while the others secretly write clues for a shared hidden word. All answers are well-known proper nouns, including celebrities, places, movies, and brands. However, clues must follow strict limitations: players may only use dictionary words, and may not use proper nouns, numerals, punctuation, contractions, rhymes, or other shortcuts.

Once all clues are written, they are revealed from shortest to longest by word count (with ties broken by the number of letters in the first word). After each clue, the guesser has a single chance to name the answer. If the guesser answers correctly, the player whose clue led to the correct guess wins the card.

Players take turns as the guesser over a set number of rounds, and the player who has won the most cards at the end of the game wins.

—description from the designer

Kazoku - Family is Everything is a deck building game for 2-4 players.  The rules are simple but strategy is deep and engaging.  Players start with the same value of cards purchasing higher value cards to be used in future turns.  The family cards "Father, Daughter, Robot" are worth the most. Decide, Do I buy the card, score the card or prevent my opponent from scoring.  If all 3 family cards are scored, a family bonus is scored which can be the difference between wining and losing.

Prepare yourself for a chaotic new party game, based on Dropout's hit game show, where the rules keep changing.

Split into two teams and compete in 3 mini games!

In BINGO, each team is given a bingo board and 5 Thing cards containing words the opposing team must get their teammates to guess before time runs out. Fill your bingo board with words you think the opposite team will using during their turn, and guess your own team's Things without using the words your opponents predicted you would use.

Name a Number is an oblivious bidding game where you take turns bidding for a task, without knowing what it is. The player with the winning bid takes the card, reads it, and either accepts the challenge or passes to the other team.

In Sam Says, each team has 3 minutes to get their teammates to guess as many words as possible- but with each new word you must follow a new Sam Says rule!

Truckin' '76 takes you back to the year 1976 and the days of CB radios, truck driver culture and the 55 mile per hour national speed limit.

Truckin' '76 is a four-barrel diesel-fueled race against time and state troopers. You've got 24 hours to get from Texarkana to Atlanta with a payload of domestic beer, and you've got choices. Get there FAST and collect the "express bonus," or take the scenic route and pick up extra payload and truckin' perks.

Designed to be played solo, there's also a two-player version of Truckin' '76

—description from the designer

Are you smarter than your family members?
This is a quiz-style family game suitable for mixed age groups. It’s designed as a light competitive activity.
The game includes 800 questions covering a wide range of topics. If you don’t know the answer, you can guess between A, B, C, or D.

Aldarra: New Empire adds additional rules and content modules for the base game Aldarra and the Savage Skies expansion.

Pick and play from 5 new modules added to the game to create an even deeper tactical and social game experience with trade mechanics, new resource types, directives, and more.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Ascend the Shinra Tower: Final Fantasy VII
description:
Ascend the swaying Shinra Tower and confront the crisis threatening the planet.

Inspired by FINAL FANTASY VII, this thrilling cooperative balance board game has you and your teammates working together to ascend the unstable Shinra Tower.

Stack “Floor Tiles” atop “Wall Tiles” of varying heights to build upward, level by level.
As the Shinra Tower rises, it gradually begins to tilt, becoming increasingly unstable…
Maintaining balance through teamwork and discussion becomes the key to success in this cooperative challenge.
Foes who stand in Cloud’s way will appear one after another within the Shinra Tower.
Borrow the strength of your allies to overcome these enemies as Cloud continues his ascent toward the top. If you can prevent the Shinra Tower from collapsing and help Cloud reach the 8th floor, you win the game — but the final challenge still awaits…
Will you be able to overcome your nemesis Sephiroth and save the planet from crisis?

All newly created artwork featured on the package and on the game’s wooden components is fully supervised by Tetsuya Nomura, the original character designer and story concept creator of FINAL FANTASY VII.

Cloud, along with familiar allies such as Tifa and Aerith and the foes they face, appears on wooden pieces featuring charming, storybook‑style artwork—a fresh reimagining of the FFVII characters created for this board game.

In Nebulirium Overlords, players take on the role of leaders of newly formed nations, each based on a unique biome—deserts, jungles, mountains, tundras, and more. As they develop their civilizations, players must carefully manage resources to not only grow their economies but balance technological progress.

However, just as their nations begin to flourish, an unexpected crisis emerges—an onslaught of alien invaders from across the Galaxy arrive to conquer Earth! These extraterrestrial forces have specific desires for Earth’s resources, targeting nations that possess the materials they crave. Players must adapt by manipulating their own economies and influencing their neighbors’ economies to not become the target of the worst of these invaders. In some cases, attracting more benign alien factions that can aid in the defense against the worst of the Nebulirium forces is the optimal move.

Players simultaneously select cards to pass left and right and then select one card to complement the two cards they received. Each card has three sections (Department of Health, Department of Agriculture, and Department of Defense). Player simultaneously select one department on each card to obtain the indicated resources (Military, Energy, Minerals, Food, Population).

It is critical to properly support your nation. If you are not able to support a specific part of your nation, you are able to explore, which provides increased flexibility on future turns.

In order to grow your Population, you need a sufficient Food Supply. To grow food, you need Minerals to build heavy equipment. To mine the Minerals, you need an extensive amount of Energy sources. To maintain control of those Energy sources, you need the proper Military protection.

After gaining resources, random aliens attack Earth and become an overlord of the nation who has the most or least of the alien's desired resource. After 9 aliens have arrived on Earth, whoever has the most points wins. Each resource is worth a point, aliens add or subtract points, and location objectives provide additional points.

Each game is completely different as only 9 of 26 Aliens are used each game and the order in which they reach Earth completely changes their impact. Some Aliens are better earlier in the game, some are better later in the game, and some are always overbearing.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Grimdark Future: Sirius Chronicles – Volume II
description:
Earth is but a distant memory and humanity struggles to survive in the Sirius Sector, a new galaxy filled with epic conflicts, mighty heroes, and daring adventurers. Now they join in the conflicts of many species and factions in the battle for  survival and supremacy.

Sirius Chronicles Vol. 2 collects 1 short stories set in the worlds of Grimdark Future, and gives you a glimpse of the life and conflicts of the lives and people of the Sirius Sector.

Bring these tales to your game table as well. Each story includes a custom mission for Grimdark Future or Grimdark Future: Firefight to let you play out these personal conflicts and bring more variety to your games.

Out among the stars, how will your story be remembered in this Grimdark Future?

Included Stories & Missions:

     Refuge by Magnus Crain


Even though the missions are designed for Grimdark Future and Grimdark Future: Firefight, you can play them with the rules for any of our other games (with slight adjustments).

Note that to play the included you’re going to need the core rules for Grimdark Future as well as some army lists, which you can find for free on the publisher's website.

boardgame: Slavering Maw-Blade of the Ancient Cannibal Necromancer
description:
This player made supplement adds 1 Endgame Awakened Evil card to the original CAVE EVIL game. Players can aim to obtain the forbidden Maw-Blade from The Pit and win the game by total Necromantic slaughter!

This Supplement was made by Cave Evil player Joseph Hughes, and is Print and Play in style, players cut out new Awakened Evil and Maw-Blade cards, plus Standee and go to battle.

Mono:Solo, for short, is a solitaire card game of survival, trust, and redemption at the eleventh-hour.

You are Commander Dave Bowman aboard Stanley Kubrick's legendary Discovery One and the last conscious crew member on a ship bound for Jupiter's moon, Io. Your onboard computer, HAL 9000, just killed a crew member, while you watched helplessly, and you disconnected HAL to survive. Now HAL is running on 1968 base code, and corrupted firmware patches from Earth are your only hope of rebuilding him into something you can trust with your life. You need HAL operational. Because something ancient is already aboard your ship, and it's been infecting HAL's logic to get to you, to become its golden specimen.

You only have twelve turns to escape the Monolith’s invisible traps—where the ship’s location cards conceal mission-critical tasks and hidden objects, while upgrading HAL, and hunting for the Monolith’s secrets before it finds you and literally, seals your fate, seven ways from Sunday.

One question: can you ever again trust the HAL 9000 you had to destroy, to save yourself?

—description from the designer

boardgame: Age of Fantasy: Tales of Tyria – Volume II
description:
The stories of the lands of Tyria are the stories of war, triumph, courage, struggle and sacrifice. After the collapse of an era of peace and order, the inhabitants face many dangers, mysteries and opportunities as they struggle to survive on a shattered continent and face new, powerful, and unknown forces.

Tales of Tyria Vol. 2 collects 1 short stories set in this world and gives you a glimpse of the life and challenges of this new and dangerous era.

Bring these tales to your game table as well. Each story includes a custom mission for Age of Fantasy or Age of Fantasy: Skirmish to let you play out these personal conflicts and bring more variety to your games.

When the stakes are personal, how will you forge new tales in this Age of Fantasy?

Included Stories & Missions:

     The Queen's Burden by Robbie McGuinness


Even though the missions are designed for Age of Fantasy and Age of Fantasy: Skirmish, you can play them with the rules for any of our other games (with slight adjustments).

—description from the publisher

Rhêtorikê is a rhetoric-based debate game in which players build a debate using playing cards. The game is designed for 2 to 4 players aged 13 to 77, and each session lasts about 30 minutes. Players take turns speaking and must formulate arguments to score points. Speakers can play three types of argument cards: demonstrative (green), deliberative (blue), and eristic (red). These arguments require players to articulate their side’s thesis using specific rhetorical figures, forms of sophistry, persuasion techniques, and by incorporating as many vocabulary cards as their strength allows.

This is a printable murder mystery case file for 1-4 players. Players must use a variety of evidence and complete 4 objectives to find the correct suspect. It's a medium difficulty case file for ages 15+.

After the murder of Jonathan Monroe in his home, you are called out to assist investigators in finding the killer. What looks like a regular burglary, might be something much more devious. Solve clues and puzzles, interrogate suspects, and unravel a web of deceit and betrayal as you try to find out who committed this heinous crime.

—description from the publisher

A printable murder mystery case file for 1-4 players. Players must use a variety of evidence and complete 6 objectives to find the correct suspect. This is a puzzle-heavy game. It's designed as a hard difficulty case file for ages 15+.

After a hiker is found murdered on a popular hiking trail, you are called out to investigate. Authorities think it's an accident but keen-eyed detectives might find that it's not.

—description from the publisher

A printable murder mystery case file for 1-4 players. Players take on the role of a detective and must use the evidence provided to complete 3 objectives to find the killer. This is a deduction-focused game. It's a medium-hard difficulty case file for ages 15+.

After the murder of a local waiter, you are called out to assist investigators in finding the killer. Solve clues and puzzles, interrogate suspects, and follow evidence closely to try to find out who the killer is.

—description from the publisher

A printable murder mystery case file for 1-4 players. Players take on the role of a detective and must use the evidence provided to complete 3 fun objectives to find the murderer. This is a deduction-focused game. It is a medium difficulty case file for ages 15+.

A murder has taken place onboard a luxury cruise liner – the Harmony Seascape. The victim, a financial advisor, was found submerged in his room’s bathtub after the cruise ship docked at port. Poison was found in his system, but not at a lethal dose. You are tasked at finding out what really happened to him.

—description from the publisher

A fun printable murder mystery case file for 1-4 players set in the 1800s. Players take on the role of a detective and must use the evidence provided to complete 7 objectives to find the killer. This is a deduction-focused game. It is a medium-hard difficulty case file for ages 15+.

When Lord Samuel Evercroft is found dead in his study, you are brought in as a consulting detective to try find out if he was murdered. You’ll gather evidence, interview suspects, and piece together a complex web of family drama, financial motives, and past grievances.

—description from the publisher

A fun printable murder mystery case file for 1-4 players set in the 1930s Turkey. Players take on the role of a detective and must use the evidence provided to complete 6 objectives to find the killer. This is a deduction-focused game. It is a medium-hard difficulty case file for ages 15+.

Dr. Harold Fulbright, an American scientist who is on the verge of a groundbreaking discovery in synthetic fuels, is found dead in the the Tulip King Hotel. With only a 10-minute window of opportunity for the crime, suspicion falls on the five other people staying at the hotel. The motive could be financial, personal, or maybe even political. As the investigator, you’ll work through six objectives, analyzing witness statements, hidden notes, coded messages, and fingerprints to solve this murder.

—description from the publisher

Eighty square tiles are tossed into a bag. Each tile displays one of three colors, one of three shapes, and one of three numbers. On their turn, a player draws tiles to refill their hand to four tiles and then plays up to three of those tiles in a straight line onto a grid of previously played tiles so that the shapes, numbers, and colors of each line created or extended are either all the same or all different. Score a point for each tile in a line that is created or extended. Score bonuses for playing three tiles and for creating lines of three tiles. Play until the bag empties, and one player plays all their tiles. Then, subtract the number of every other player's leftover tiles from their score and add the sum of those tile to the score of the player who went out. Most points wins.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Jazz, Moonshine and Murder: A 1920s Speakeasy Murder Mystery
description:
A murder mystery dinner party game for 8-20 people. There will be 3 murders committed during the party. Players will have to find out who the killer is, as well as work out the secrets that all other players are keeping. This in an improv style game. There are no scripts, only talking points and actions that players can take to earn points. The person with the highest points wins.

All 20 characters are gender-neutral, so your friends can play as anyone. There are no romantic relationship or actions in this game, so it's safe for work and personal parties.

This is an interactive murder mystery party game filled with flappers, mobsters, bootleggers, and socialites, where everyone has something to hide.

—description from the publisher

This is murder mystery dinner party game for 8-20 people. There will be 3 murders committed during the party. Players will have to find out who the killer is, as well as work out the secrets that all other players are keeping. This in an improv style game. There are no scripts, only talking points and actions that players can do for fun. It includes everything you need to host a murder mystery dinner party game

All 20 characters are gender-neutral, so your friends can play as anyone. There are no romantic relationship or actions in this game, so it's safe for work and personal parties.

You'll take on the role of Alex Bailey, an adventurer in search of an island that seemingly appears and disappears of its own accord. You’ve invited some of the best minds to your home to help you find a way to reach the island, but not everyone has the best intentions. Each guest has their own secret reasons for accepting your invitation. You never know who you can trust. The story takes place before your intended expedition, as you invite guests over to your home to choose who will come with you.

—description from the publisher

Forbidden Quest is a 2-Player trading card game that is played on a gameboard consisting of 9 square regions organized in a 3x3 array. Both players build and use their own 50-card deck that includes creature cards, weapon and defense cards, and sorcerer and relic cards. During play, creature cards are added to the game board, equipped with weapon and defense cards, and moved between the gameboard regions to battle opponent creatures and collect crystal cards.  You win if you eliminate your opponent's creatures from the board and collect all 9 crystal cards.

—description from the designer

Butterscotch is a light-weight, fixed-partnerships climbing game for exactly 4 players. It has a short meld menu to keep the game simple for new players, but with enough variety to entertain veterans of the genre.

The game is very much focused on going out first and, hopefully, helping your partner go out second. The first team to 11 points wins.
family: Organizations: The Game Artisans of Canada

description:
All tokens with images are shuffled face down and the cards with the athlets are put face up on the table so that every child can choose one. This is the starting card. The first player takes 2 tokens. If at least one of the two tokens matches the colour of the athlet's card, the child reads the name of the object and adds it to the card and goes to the next card.  
The child who manages to be the first one to be back on his starting card wins.

boardgame: Wickie und die starken Männer: Wicke und das Rätsel von Flake
description:
Wickie und das Rätsel von Flake (Wickie and the Riddle of Flake) is a memory-based board game for 2 to 4 players, aged 5–12, focused on finding Viking tokens to fill their player board.
Shuffle all 44 round search tiles and spread them face-down in the center of the table. Sort the 40 vikings card in descending order so that "1" is on the top card. The youngest player starts, and reveals 4 tokens. He selects one of the vikings of the tokens and takes the corresponding viking card with the "1" and puts it on his board. He turns all flipped tiles face-down again. Then it's the next player's turn. If any player wants to take the viking with a "2" or more, he needs to reveal the corresponding number of vikings. 
The game continues until one player fills all 10 slots on their board. This player wins the game.

Each player takes 8 gnomes of one colour. The 24 garden fence pieces are placed next to the board.
The youngest player starts the game and then the game takes turns. The game starts with the players taking turns to place one garden fence per turn on an empty space of the board. Each meadow (green circle) is bounded by 4 sides. This means that 4 garden fences are needed to get the meadow. It's important to pay attention to where the other player places his garden fences. If you have enclosed a green field with 4 fences, you have won the field and are allowed to put a gnome of your colour there. Now, it's the other player's turn again.
As soon as all green fields are surrounded by garden fences, the game ends. The winner is the player who was able to place the most gnomes on the board.

boardgame: Grimdark Future: Star Quest – Into the Frontier: Narrative Campaign
description:
Into the Frontier is a narrative campaign for Grimdark Future: Star Quest that takes you on an adventure starting on Tugator, the famed satellite at the edge of the Frontier. You meet your contact at a seedy bar in the station, and as things are being explained, your mark warks in the door...

This game expansion for Grimdark Future: Star Quest takes players on an adventure across 12 missions which are tied together by an exciting story of epic proportions.

Even though this narrative campaign was written with Human Defense Force as enemies in mind, it is easy to imagine this type of clash to be happening against any of the other factions in the universe, so feel free to swap them out with whatever army you have available to play.

Note that to play this campaign you’re going to need the core rules for Grimdark Future: Star Quest as well as heroes and enemies, which you can find for free on the publisher's website.

The main villain of this campaign is available for purchase as 3D print files on the One Page Rules MyMiniFactory store.

Into the Frontier is book 1 out of 5 in the Chrono Shards saga for Grimdark Future: Star Quest. Make sure to check out the rest of the books in the saga to complete this epic narrative.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Grimdark Future: Star Quest – Escaping New Orzuk: Narrative Campaign
description:
Escape from New Orzuk takes you on an adventure where your hunt for the Chrono Shards has left you stranded on an Orc colony, as you must escape the clutches of tyrannical Rurthdeg and his mysterious Elven companion.

This game expansion for Grimdark Future: Star Quest takes players on an adventure across 12 missions which are tied together by an exciting story of epic proportions.

Even though this narrative campaign was written with Orc Marauders as enemies in mind, it is easy to imagine this type of clash to be happening against any of the other factions in the universe, so feel free to swap them out with whatever army you have available to play.

To play this campaign you’re going to need the core rules for Grimdark Future: Star Quest as well as heroes and enemies, which you can find for free on onepagerules website.

The main villain of this campaign is available for purchase as 3D print files on the One Page Rules MyMiniFactory store.

Escape from New Orzuk is book 2 out of 5 in the Chrono Shards saga for Grimdark Future: Star Quest. Make sure to check out the rest of the books in the saga to complete this epic narrative.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Grimdark Future: Star Quest – By Crystal's Glow: Narrative Campaign
description:
By Crystal’s Glow takes you on an adventure that puts you in pursuit of the legendary adventurer Ellisia as she pursues the second shard to a long-abandoned irradiated mining facility which has been reclaimed by Goblin scavengers.

This game expansion for Grimdark Future: Star Quest takes players on an adventure across 6 missions which are tied together by an exciting story of epic proportions.

Even though this narrative campaign was written with Goblin Reclaimers as enemies in mind, it is easy to imagine this type of clash to be happening against any of the other factions in the universe, so feel free to swap them out with whatever army you have available to play.

To play this campaign you’re going to need the core rules for Grimdark Future: Star Quest as well as heroes and enemies, which you can find for free on the onepagerules site.

The main villain of this campaign is available for purchase as 3D print files on the One Page Rules MyMiniFactory store.

By Crystal’s Glow is book 3 out of 5 in the Chrono Shards saga for Grimdark Future: Star Quest. Make sure to check out the rest of the books in the saga to complete this epic narrative.

—description from the publisher

Granary is a card bidding/drafting and polyomino placement game for 1-4 players. So just what is a granary?

Acorn woodpeckers are amazing birds, they stash acorns in dead trees called "Granaries" by pecking holes in them and then inserting acorns into them. One Granary may have up to 50,000 holes in it.

They also live in small cooperative flocks, each having specific duties to the flock. One might gather up the acorns, another might organize and stash the acorns, another might guard the tree from animal acorn thieves.

In this game you are an acorn woodpecker, setting up a new Granary that you found, you will be harvesting acorns and stash them in your Granary while also enticing and feeding bird friends, thereby convincing them to join your flock. You are competing with other players to be the best fed with the best flock by the start of winter (the end of the game).

There are four phases to the game:

     Bid Phase: Players will play one card face up from their hands in turn order attempting to get the Harvest card they want based on bid value of the card they played. (Highest gets highest, 2nd highest gets the 2nd highest, 3rd highest, the 3rd highest, Lowest gets lowest)
     Harvest Phase: Each player gets a card from the Harvest board based on their bid number.
     Feed & Stash: Players apply the polyomino and collect loose acorns from the harvest, and put them on Bird Friends and/or on their Granary. Get a new bird friend immediately from your personal discard pile if the one you have is completed.
     Reset phase: Bid cards now become Harvest cards for the next round.


—description from the publisher

M.A.S.S IMPACT is a tactical level game of man-to-man combat in a dystopian future. Players manage a squad or full platoon of heavily armed and armored soldiers as they navigate through blasted terrain to accomplish their mission. This could be to capture or hold a position, break through the enemy lines, or simply destroy enemy soldiers.

Players advance their soldiers across a grid of map points that indicate the type of terrain at that spot of the map. This grid allows for much more maneuver within a much smaller space. Ten different 11x17 inch geomorphic maps, allowing for hundreds of different battlefield configurations.

Each soldier is tracked with his or her own data card, using dry-erase markers, allowing the capabilities of each soldier to remain hidden until engaged. Ten different soldier types, each with its own abilities can be selected as part of a squad, through twelve different scenarios. An included “design-your-own” system makes it easy to create custom scenarios.

The game's action is managed through the use of a deck of "action cards" that provide quick, dice-less combat results as well as random events.

In addition to a straight-forward set of movement and combat rules, the game includes several optional rules including “bouncing” to target enemy soldiers over blocking terrain, stealth movement, drones, mines, squad command and morale, and even guidelines on how to play the game with miniatures.

—description from the publisher'

With the greatest artists of the Renaissance at your disposal, provide them with flashes of inspiration in Color, Forms, and Ideas as they being their masterpieces

—description from the back of the box

The Great Air Race is played with cards, which represent the player’s progress through the various stages of the race.

A race is made up of a number of Stage cards (usually 3-5), laid out to form a course. Players must traverse this course by playing Flight cards, each of which denotes a certain amount of Progress towards this goal.

The first player to reach the finish line at the end of the race is the winner.

—description from the publisher

Akta Tajemnic: Kobieta nad stawem (Polish for "Files of Secrets: The Woman by the Pond") is a cooperative detective game for 1 to 10 players, designed by Marcin Myszka, the host of the Polish true-crime podcast Kryminatorium. The game is published in Polish only.
The case is set in 1985 in Poznań, in communist-era Poland. The body of a young waitress is found in a park by a pond, with one hand missing. Evidence at the scene suggests the killing was not random. Players take the role of investigators trying to reconstruct what happened and identify the perpetrator.
The box contains seven sealed envelopes filled with physical case materials: police reports, witness statements, interrogation transcripts, photographs, letters, handwritten notes, a newspaper clipping, a map, a restaurant menu, and a transit schedule. A companion website guides players through the investigation without spoilers and provides audio versions of selected interrogations, which are also included on paper. Players decide what to examine and in what order, then submit their accusation through the website at the end of the case.
There is no board, no dice, no score track. The game is driven entirely by reading the documents, comparing testimonies, spotting inconsistencies, and building a theory of the crime. It can be played solo, but is designed as a group activity with players splitting the materials between them.
A single playthrough takes around three hours. The case is one-shot and is not meant to be replayed once solved.
The publisher rates the game 18+ due to graphic content and mature themes related to a violent crime against a woman.
Format-wise it sits in the same family as Chronicles of Crime, Detective: A Modern Crime Board Game, and Kryminauci, but without an app-only structure - the bulk of the experience is physical documents, with the website acting as a guide rather than a primary interface.

1863 Marsala Express is an 18xx railway and stock market game set in Southern Italy during the great age of rail expansion, where players take the role of investors shaping the development of Sicily and Calabria. You will found companies, speculate on shares, and build rail networks linking inland resources to strategic ports. The setting is not just historical flavor: key locations like Palermo, Catania, and Reggio Calabria—and especially the Strait of Messina—drive both geography and strategy, forcing players to think in terms of real economic routes and constraints.

Gameplay follows the classic 18xx structure of alternating Stock Rounds and Operating Rounds, but with several elegant twists. Players buy and sell shares, control companies as presidents, lay track, run trains, and decide whether to pay or withhold dividends. A standout feature is the simplified dividend system, where all payouts are handled in clean multiples of 10, eliminating tedious calculations while preserving meaningful financial decisions. Ports play a crucial role, boosting route revenue by 50%, while the Strait of Messina introduces unique rules: early in the game it acts as a barrier or costly crossing, later becoming a seamless connection that can redefine entire networks.

One of the defining innovations of 1863 Marsala Express is its powerful Automa system, designed to fully replace a human player. Unlike many solo variants, the Automa is deeply integrated into both stock market behavior and operational play, with adaptive traits and difficulty levels that make it suitable not only for solo play but also for enhancing 2-player games. It makes meaningful financial decisions, builds networks with purpose, and competes for control, creating a dynamic and challenging opponent.

The game ends when the bank breaks or a player goes bankrupt, and victory goes to the wealthiest investor—combining cash and portfolio value. Included in the box is also 1850jr, a streamlined mini-game focused on Sicily alone, offering a shorter and more accessible experience while retaining the core tension of the system. Together, they provide both a deep, strategic flagship game and a fast-playing alternative set in the same rich historical world.

—description from the publisher

Maids Hotel is a cooperative game for 1 to 4 players in which you work together to keep the hotel in good shape while facing unexpected events, problems, and interact with many attractive, distracting maids.

You take on the roles of characters who begin to notice that the world around them does not behave as it should. After eating a fruit from a mysterious tree, your eyes closed for just a moment, but since then something has changed. Now, whenever you look away, small details seem to shift. Rooms may appear different than they were moments before or have strange layouts, almost like something from a dream. And yet, everything still feels real.

In these unusual circumstances, you arrive at a hotel where you will spend the next three days, taking care of its atmosphere, cash, tidiness, fame, and relations.

You have three days to prepare the hotel for a final meeting with the head manager, who, with a bit of luck, may show you the way back to your own world.

Be careful, though, because the dangerous Hagatha is always nearby and waiting for your mistakes.

boardgame: Dossiers Criminels Pocket: Qui a tué Isabella Vega ?
description:
One year after the death of contestant Isabella Vega on the reality TV show Casa Olympia, the investigation is reopened! Should the accident theory be called into question? Dive behind the scenes of the show to find out.

Reconstruct the timeline of events, compare testimonies, analyze documents, solve puzzles and… accuse the murderer!

Play solo or in a group—work together and find the solution by examining every card and making the right deductions.

No rules to read—gather around a table and start playing right away!

Difficulty: 2/3

boardgame: The Princess in the Tower: Rapunzel
description:
The Princess in the Tower: Rapunzel is a cooperative puzzle game where players work together to help the Prince climb the tower and reach Rapunzel before the Witch.
Each round, players reveal a Tangram card showing a shape to complete. Using all seven Tangram pieces, players must recreate the shape within a limited time. The game is played against the clock, encouraging teamwork, spatial reasoning, and quick thinking.
If the players successfully complete the shape in time, the Prince climbs one step up the tower. If they fail, the Witch advances instead. The race continues until one reaches the top—either rescuing Rapunzel or preventing the Prince from succeeding.
With multiple difficulty levels, the game offers scalable challenges for different ages and skill levels, making it both educational and engaging.

—description from the publisher

In Fliegen kriegen, you’re running a cow bar. However there’s one big problem: flies everywhere. Cards are played to a central area, actions must be called out quickly, and suddenly cards are flying across the table. Change directions, pass the dung token around, and keep your cool while chaos unfolds. In the end, the player with the fewest flies wins - probably the one who handled the madness best.

—description from the publisher

In Fauch & Fertig, young dragons must take responsibility and carefully manage their eggs. You collect cards, combine values and colors, and fulfill conditions to place your eggs. The better your cards work together, the faster you progress—but shared goals and clever decisions add an extra layer of strategy. The first player to place all their eggs wins this accessible yet tactical dragon showdown.

—description from the publisher

Two promo cards included with the trove chest. One card is a standard "Loot", the other is a "Trove Loot".

- Loot "Magnetic Bone Key" (1 time usage)
In battle get 4 bones
OR
After a lock pick attempt you get 3 additional attempts

- Trove-Loot "Future Back Injury"
Put the card besides the battle map. Place 4 standard loot besides each other faced up under it. The "Future Back Injury" can hold 4 loot (heavy counts as 1).
During rest: Change your loot with loot below this card as often as you wish.
During battle: On your turn you may take any loot from under this card and place it in your loot area.

Solo dungeon crawler board game where Elder Ones seized the dungeon and you need to get rid of them, gathering runes and Necronomicon to summon the Eldest One and fight him in the final encounter.

The game takes place in a very classic dungeon full of goblins, orcs, and undead. Visuals are strongly inspired by good old PC dungeon crawlers and Fighting Fantasy aesthetics.

Gameplay is simple, you just flip dungeon cards and resolve encounters, mostly with rolling dice.

You have 10 characters to choose from and there is around 120 encounters and 90 items.

—description from the designer

In Tonaludo, players enter a playful musical world where notes, rhythm and quick thinking come together in a set of accessible card games. The game is designed around basic music reading skills, but presents them through familiar card-game mechanisms, making music practice feel like a lively challenge rather than an exercise.

The main game, The Note Duel, is a competitive game for two players. Each player builds a row of note cards and plays cards by matching color or symbol. To score points, players must correctly name the notes they play. Action cards add interaction by letting players swap, block or influence each other’s cards, creating a light tactical duel where musical knowledge and clever timing both matter.

The box also includes two solo challenges focused on speed and recognition. In these modes, players try to identify notes as quickly and accurately as possible, improving their fluency through repeated short challenges. Together, the different game modes allow players to practise note reading in both competitive and individual ways.

In The Note Duel, the game is played over three rounds. Players score points by correctly identifying notes and using their cards effectively. After the third round, the player with the highest total score wins.

—description from the publisher

This expansion blends the iconic survival challenges of the “Squid Game” franchise with the established Zombicide gameplay loop, adding new threats and scenarios where players must outwit both relentless zombies and ruthless game-like hazards.
