The game was designed, tested, and developed by myself, with the goal of creating a family game that is easy to explain but offers real strategic choices.

—description from the designer

Panzertrain! is a light armored train wargame set in an alternative WWI aftermath. A simultaneous activation system and minimal dice rolling cement this game as a fast, brutal, and tactical experience of railway warfare.

Build your own custom armored train and lead it to victory! With a wide variety of armed and support wagons, optional rules for special crew members and diverse victory conditions, the game is highly customizable and perfect for high-paced tactical combat.

The bare minimum you need is the rulebook, a sheet of paper, and a pair of pencils. However, the game also provides options for miniature play, multiple factions, and 3+ player matches, ensuring no two battles are ever the same!

—description from the designer

Explore microscopic ecosystems!

Based on the original food web building game, Ecologies, this game looks at microscopic food webs and the amazing creatures that live in them. Each biome is from a different miniature environment or microhabitat. Using real science, and in collaboration with both a microbiologist and an award-winning science illustrator, we look at fascinating life forms that are usually overlooked.

Designed by a biology teacher, the game includes 78 unique organisms in 7 micro biomes, and features beautiful scientific art. (108 cards total)

The goal is to build healthy food webs in different biomes. If you create a healthy ecology you are rewarded with the perks and powers of that biome. Each organism card lists what it eats and what it’s eaten by. Cards are arranged with producers on the bottom, and predators on top. There is a trade mechanic similar to Catan where players trade with others based on what their individual needs are. It is a race to get 12 victory points and different organisms give different points. There are biotic/abiotic factor cards that can affect opponents and different strategies such as focusing on one biome, going for perks and bonuses, or disrupting other ecologies.

The game takes inspiration from titles like Settlers of Catan, Agricola, and Dominion. The gameplay is rooted in authentic science, as it is used in the classroom to teach real concepts to science students. While it is scientific enough for the classroom, it is also strategic enough for any game night.

The points system is based on ecological trophic levels. The producers (plants) are worth less and as you make your way up the pyramid organisms are worth more as it takes a lot of energy to sustain them. In real life only roughly 10% of the energy makes it from one level to the next, meaning you have to have acres of land and hundreds of animals just to sustain one apex predator. There is a 24 page rulebook included with the card game that also gives an overview of the science.

—description from the designer

boardgame: All True Believers: The False Prophets
description:
False Prophets deepens every system in All True Believers: new Disciples spanning categories you haven't seen yet, new Crises of Faith, and entirely new Faith card mechanics that rewrite the rules of allegiance...
Including the False Prophet, who at first appears to be a standard Defector, but plays for no one but themselves and those they pull into their new sect.

Devious Artifacts introduces a secret parallel game that surfaces near the end of the game. Powerful objects dealt face-down at the start, each with abilities that can protect, manipulate, or eliminate.
Some players will bluff that they shouldn't be targeted. Others will try to slip by unnoticed. Eventually there's only one way to find out who has which.

Core Space: Evolution is a sci-fi miniatures board game in which you control a spaceship crew trying to make a living on the streets of a cyberpunk city, while investigating a new threat. The Purge, a race of humanoid machines thought long defeated, are back, and are seemingly self aware...

The game takes place on the highly- detailed 3D board included in the box. Your Traders must explore the area using the unique 'real search' system, fulfil their objectives, salvage what they can and then escape.

The campaign system tracks your crew from game to game, developing their skills, upgrading their equipment and immersing you in their story.

Evolution is the second edition of the Core Space game system, originally launched in 2019. It has been reworked from the ground up, introducing new mechanics and components to deliver the same immersive experience with streamlined gameplay, while remaining compatible with the existing miniatures and terrain.

—description from the publisher

In a series of missions, players terraform new planets and discover new cards and mechanics. Winning a mission makes you governor of that planet and earns you extra points for the final mission where the overall winner will be decided.

Based on the familiar mechanics of Terraforming Mars, Legacy of Mars adds mechanics such as melting glaciers, worker resources, population, unlocking powerful innovation cards, and developing your corporation over time with more and more (prelude-like) development cards.

Each mission is set on a new planet, starting with a new side of Mars (needing 15 ocean tiles). New parameters, new mechanics, and new card pools mean that this is essentially several games in one box.

This campaign is fully replayable and it is about what we take with us from having terraformed Mars - the Legacy of Mars.

—description from the designer

Pod Zmajevim Suncem (Under the Dragon’s Sun) is a tactical card game in which players take on the roles of Dragon Lords, selecting Hero, Dragon, and Event cards to build the most powerful army. The goal of the game is to collect as much gold as possible through dragon cards, battles, treasury tokens, and event cards. Every decision and move can shift the course of the game, making each playthrough a unique challenge.
The player with the most points at the end, earned by controlling the most valuable dragons and collecting gold from the treasury, wins

The core idea behind the game was to create a simple rules system with deep strategic depth and numerous decision-making possibilities.
Thanks to this approach, the game becomes accessible to a wide audience and easy to understand from the very first play. However, the heart of the experience lies in uncovering its mystery—through exploring new tactics, card synergies, and different ways to utilize effects in order to outsmart your opponent.
Every game setup is different, meaning players must learn how to adapt their plans depending on changing situations. The game acts as a large strategic puzzle, giving you the opportunity to be creative and always find your own unique path to victory.
With new cards constantly appearing on the table, player decisions can shift within seconds, resulting in a highly dynamic and consistently engaging experience throughout the entire game.

—description from the designer

The Rabbit of the Village is organizing a masked party for all his chicken friends. You will play the role of a doorman at the entrance to the party. The first player to get rid of all their cards, which represent their guest list, is the winner.

You must match either the same color or the same chicken as the last card played, but everyone plays at the same time—there are no turns. You will flap your arms to play multiple cards, shout “Chickie Chickie” to stop the fox from entering, and sneak intruders into the party to block those of your opponents. When you are almost finished with your guest list, you must also make chicken noises.

Chaotic, absurd, and fun.

—description from the designer

Buyout is 3–6 player semi-cooperative bidding and tableau-building game where players take the role of portfolio managers at a private equity firm facing an imminent hostile takeover and mass layoff. When the Buyout Trigger hits, players must collectively pool enough money to outbid the takeover, but each player also needs to contribute their fair share and secretly complete a hidden (potentially hostile) Performance Goal to avoid getting laid off.

Players will race to acquire income-generating Assets and favors from Contacts to stay ahead of the group. The tension between ruthless competition during card acquisition and the forced cooperation of the endgame counteroffer is the heart of the game: the same players you just stabbed in the back are the ones you need on your side when it counts.

—description from the publisher

Cross Universe is a two player deck-building card game.

In the game, both players compete with a deck constructed beforehand and have 1000 life points each. The goal of the game is to reduce the opponent's life points to 0 through the abilities of one's cards or combat between the cards.

Decks can be constructed from the over 500 available cards, which are divided into spells, items and units. One unit card from the deck is chosen to be the player's 'partner card' at the start of the game and always remains in play. This gives more consistent access to that card's abilities than the rest of the deck which which gets shuffled and is then drawn from throughout play.

Reaching the top floor as quickly as possible may not always be the right strategy. Players infiltrate the building, bypass security, and try to collect money. The order in which players enter the same floor is crucial, as the rewards vary accordingly. While those who enter earlier tend to earn more, there are also advantages for players who arrive later.
Visually, the game looks very impressive. Mechanically, it seems to be a worker placement game at its core. You send your three workers to different areas and assign them specific roles. While making these decisions, you also need to anticipate and read your opponents’ moves.

The game is set on the planet Home, in a post-cyberpunk universe.
After undergoing exponential technological evolution and crossing numerous ethical boundaries, humanity has ultimately depleted the majority of the planet’s resources. Betrayed by their ruling caste and cornered by the collapse of the environment, humans have implemented drastic solutions to survive. From the independence of A.I. to the modification of their own genome, humanity is pushed to its limits.
In Warbound, you embody the leaders of a small group of individuals trying to make their mark in a dramatically dangerous environment.
Resources are scarce, alliances are fragile. It’s up to you to tell the story of your Warband: either be a glimmer of hope in a world on the brink, or infuse more chaos and destruction into the mixt.

In Warbound, two players assemble the best possible team through the game’s highly customizable deck-building system.
As the stage is set, they converge upon the grid-based game board. The two forces collide, with each player striving to outmaneuver his adversary on the Action board. Seizing the best opportunities from the opponent can be the decisive factor between defeat and victory.
Explore the ravaged surface of the planet Home in search of valuable resources or confront the enemy around crucial objectives for your leaders.
Warbound will put your strategic skills to the test. In a world on the brink, every life counts. Charging headfirst may not be the best solution.

—description from the rulebook

boardgame: King's Favor: If It Pleases Your Majesty
description:
In King’s Favor, noble dogs scheme for royal approval by carefully managing their hands, balancing the kingdom’s treasury, and outmaneuvering rivals with timely Royal Decrees. Secret Little Bird agents grant each player a once-per-game power, adding courtly mischief, clever twists, and just the right amount of royal sabotage. *Royal Decrees create mayhem or leverage for players.

The object of the game is to have the lowest points at the end of the game. King’s Favor is typically played over 11 rounds, though players may agree to play fewer rounds.

Each round increases by 1 card, ending with 13 cards in the final round.

During each round, players try to lower the points in their hand to zero by collecting sets (minimum 3 cards) or runs (minimum 3 cards of the same suit).

To go out, all cards in your hand must be part of sets or runs. If someone succeeds, they declare, “I’m out!” and everyone else has one last turn before tallying their score. The player with the fewest points at the end wins the game.

Whoever goes out each round earns a “favor” from the King, which can be saved as VP for the end or used during a round to turn the tables.

—description from the designer

The Deluxe Kickstarter Edition includes all the exclusives described below! (Some bonuses may be made available for separate purchase.)

• THE WOLF MICRO-EXPANSION
Includes a Helper card and its very own wooden pawn!

• THE ROWBOAT MICRO-EXPANSION
This wooden rowboat gives you new versatility for getting around the market!

• FISHING DERBY MINI-EXPANSION
Includes 32 Fish cards for a push-your-luck minigame!

• DAILY SPECIALS MINI-EXPANSION
Includes 10 specials cards that let you improve and customize your Booths!

• 125 FANCY WOODEN RESOURCES
Retail edition uses punchboard tokens.

• 4 ZOOM TOKENS
When you really need to speed! (Retail edition is cardboard)

• 1 WOODEN FIREFLY TOKEN
When dusk arrives, so does this little guy! (Retail edition is cardboard)

• GAME TRAYZ INSERT & RESOURCE CADDY
Holds everything from the KS edition, including the rolled up Playmat (available as an add-on)!

- description from publisher

HexD3x is a series of solitaire card games by Stop The Killer Games in which the player matches hexagonal cards together.

Cards are flipped as sets of three, with only the top being played at a time. Edges must be matched with existing cards on the table, beginning with 1-3 "Toby" cards and circling outwards around them. If you can successfully complete your spread of cards by correctly matching 3 rings of edges (Stairs, Arch, & Line) before the 5th Obstacle card appears, you win! Four "Helper" tokens function as wilds to match edges, and Jareth appears on some cards, also functioning as a wild.

The "HexD3x" series has many versions of the game, and they all play the same way, with some of the games in the series incorporating fun tokens that "deactivate" symbols to match edges to help the Player complete their three rings.

Jerry the mouse is enjoying his birthday cake. Cream cake is his favorite food. The player who can take the most cream balls without making the cake fall is the winner. This game requires strategy and precision to win.

Attention Fleet Commander!

The blood of Arel-Zam the Conqueror is scarcely dry and already the galaxy moves to exploit the power vacuum left in the wake of her failure. Two superpowers, bitter rivals whose cold war spans millennia, present a public face of peace and diplomacy while directing violence by proxy from the shadows. The Hevelien Expansion Zone was meant to be a frontier far isolated from politics and power plays. It is instead a flashpoint taking center stage in the Great Game.

Who will you become as you lead your fleet to glory?


Fleet Command Hevelius is a miniatures agnostic tabletop miniatures game that uses a fast paced d10 system and a tight 3'x3' or 4'x4' game mat to create tense battles across entire planetary orbits, where weapon distance is measured in light seconds and turn length is measured in 30 minute increments. Ships will suffer as much damage from critical hits as from hull damage.  
Victory will be determined more by playing to objectives than just blowing holes in enemy ships.

This Quickstart Guide will help you understand whether Fleet Command Hevelius is right for you before you purchase the core rulebook.

Inside, you will find:

     Rules for movement, combat, and capturing objectives.
     Stats for ships tailor-made for a 2 player introductory experience.
     A mission outline to help you issue your first orders in the Hevelien Expansion Zone.
     Enough content to run a balanced game lasting about an hour, with assets you can reuse to run your own mission.

Blocks of Glory is a tactical simulation of Napoleonic warfare that employs the "Visual Integrated Battle Engine” (VIBE) to track and visualise fluid attributes, such as a unit’s strength, morale, and speed, using physical markers and model counts. As a result, your challenge as an army commander is not to track hit points, but to manage "Command Friction" - the physical delay between an order’s conception and its execution on the front lines. Deploy your messengers and commanders effectively to win the Battle of Waterloo. This is Warplay!

—description from the designer
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
1890s Fight Club is a tabletop sports simulation game by PLAAY Games. It simulates bare-knuckle boxing in a turn-of-the-century setting, where players manage fighters through a season of bouts at local saloons, with rankings determining matchups.

Gameplay Structure: Fighters compete in two matches per night, aiming to gain ranking points based on the quality of their opponents.

Theme: The game is set in the 1890s, offering a historically themed, gritty fighting atmosphere.

Hey there, all you bellycrawlers! It's time to answer the call of adventure!

All of Gastropodia has been shaken by the electrifying news – legends of a treasure hidden in the archipelago's wildest reaches have been confirmed. Besides piles of gold, artifacts, and cutting-edge weaponry, you can also find predatory mollusks hiding priceless pearls inside… New commanders set out on their quest, oblivious to dangers, weather anomalies, and lava-spewing volcanoes. So build a camp, prepare your weapons, and fight for a black pearl (or two…)!

Battle of Pearls is a major expansion for Snails – a dynamic game about squad battles between energized mollusks for 2-4 players. It expands the base game with unique elements: brand new factions with unique special abilities and action cards, event cards that change every game, and the "Snails for the Brave" scenario, where eliminating enemy snails is merely a secondary means to victory.

Detectives, Treasure Hunters, Vampire Women, and Samurai enter the game, the event deck introduces effects that affect the entire board, and the scenario with special elements completely changes the game's objective, offering the opportunity for new strategies.

If you thought Snails was all about ruthless combat, you'll discover that yes, it is about ruthless combat… but also about treasure!
